title: <name> and the River of Midnight Fireflies

pages.text: When <name> arrived at her grandparents’ hill village in the Western Ghats, the air smelled of wet earth and wild jasmine. The forest shimmered with green, and the sound of the river filled the valley. 'That’s our lifeline,' said Grandpa, pointing to the stream below. 'It feeds the fields, the trees, and even the fireflies at night.'

Every evening, as dusk fell, thousands of fireflies lit up the forest. <name> called it the 'River of Fireflies,' because they always seemed to float toward the river, glowing like tiny lanterns. But one night, thunder rolled — BOOM! — and rain poured like a waterfall. The next morning, the river was gone.

'The landslide from last night must have blocked it,' Grandpa said gravely. 'If the water doesn’t return, our crops will die.' <name> clenched her fists. 'There must be a way!' Later, she remembered Grandma’s story of Agni-Jharna, the Firefly Cave that controlled the river’s flow.

That night, carrying a lantern and her courage, <name> followed the trail of fireflies up the mountain. Rain drizzled softly. The rocks were slick, the air heavy with mist. 'Steady,' she told herself. A sudden roar — GRRR! — came from the bushes. A wild boar dashed across her path. She froze but didn’t run. 'It’s just scared,' she whispered.

Finally, she reached an old rope bridge swinging over a deep gorge. The river trickled far below, trapped behind fallen rocks. 'This must be it,' she said. The bridge creaked — creak… sway… creak! — as she crossed, wind tugging at her hair.

On the other side, she spotted a narrow opening in the cliff glowing faintly. Fireflies drifted in and out like living sparks. The cave walls glittered with crystals, and in the center stood a stone wheel carved with symbols — the river’s gate. But it was jammed with roots and mud.

<name> pushed, pulled, and twisted. Nothing moved. 'Think, <name>, think!' she said aloud. She guided dripping water from a stalactite onto the wheel’s grooves. Slowly, the mud softened. She braced her feet and gave one last push — CRRRRACK! The stone wheel turned, and water roared through hidden tunnels.

The cave blazed with light as fireflies whirled around her in golden spirals. 'The river!' she cried. 'It’s free!' Outside, water burst through the landslide’s edge, rushing back into its course. Down in the valley, people cheered as the fields shimmered once more.

When <name> climbed back home, soaked and muddy, Grandpa met her at the gate. 'You found the cave, didn’t you?' he said softly. She nodded, too tired to speak. Fireflies fluttered around her hands, glowing like tiny thank-yous in the dark.

That night, the forest glowed brighter than ever. Grandma whispered, 'The mountain knows your courage now, <name>.' <name> smiled. She understood — bravery wasn’t about loudness or fame. It was about standing steady, even when the world trembled.
As the rain eased and stars returned, the fireflies rose above the river — a thousand tiny suns dancing in the dark, carrying her story with them.
